content.problemStatement: There is a regular n-gon. Some points are marked on each of its sides. There are x1 point marked on the first side, x2 – on the second, …, xn – on the nth. The marked points do not coincide with the vertices of the n-gon. You can choose no more than one of the marked points from each side and form a convex non-degenerate polygon by connecting all those points with lines. Now your task is to find the number of different k-gons that can be formed that way.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer t (1 ≤ t ≤ 30) – the number of test cases. Each of the next t lines contains six integers n, k, a, b, c, m describing one test case. n (3 ≤ n ≤ 10^9) is the number of sides of the original regular polygon. k (3 ≤ k ≤ 20) is the required number of vertices of the new polygon. The number of marked points on side 1 is x1 = a (0 ≤ a < m). For i > 1 the amount on side i is xi = (b·xi‑1 + c) mod m (1 ≤ b, c, m ≤ 10^6).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the number of different convex k‑gons that can be formed, modulo 1000000007.

solution.approach: For a fixed test case let x1 … xn be the numbers of marked points on the sides. Choosing at most one point from each side and exactly k sides yields a convex k‑gon. The number of different k‑gons equals the k‑th elementary symmetric sum of the multiset {x1,…,xn}:  E_k = Σ_{1≤i1<…<ik≤n} xi1·…·xik.  The sequence xi is generated by a linear congruential generator (LCG) and therefore becomes periodic with period ≤ m (m ≤ 10^6).  Because k ≤ 20 we can keep a DP vector dp[0…k] where dp[j] is the elementary symmetric sum of degree j for the already processed prefix. Adding a new value x updates the DP in O(k):   for j = k … 1: dp[j] = (dp[j] + x·dp[j‑1]) mod MOD.  This update is a linear transformation that can be written as a (k+1)×(k+1) matrix T(x) (identity on the diagonal and x on the sub‑diagonal).  If we know the product matrix of one whole period, M = T(xp)·…·T(x1), then processing many full periods is just a matrix exponentiation: dp_after = M^{cnt} · dp_before.  Algorithm per test case:   1. Generate the sequence xi until either n values are produced or a previously seen value repeats. Store the generated values in a vector seq and remember the first index of each value (array of size m).   2. If n ≤ |seq| we simply apply the DP update for the first n values and output dp[k].   3. Otherwise we have a prefix of length pre (the part before the cycle) and a cycle of length cyc.   4. Process the prefix values with the DP update.   5. Build the matrix M for one whole cycle by multiplying the T(x) matrices of the cycle in order.   6. Raise M to the power full = (n‑pre) / cyc using fast exponentiation (log full multiplications).   7. Multiply the resulting matrix with the current dp vector.   8. Process the remaining (n‑pre) % cyc values individually.   9. The answer is dp[k] (or 0 if k > n).   Complexity: O((pre+cyc)·k) ≤ O(m·k) ≤ 2·10^7 operations for the worst case, plus O(k^3·log n) for the matrix exponentiation (k ≤ 20, negligible).  Memory consumption is O(m + k^2).
solution.timeComplexity: O(m·k + k^3·log n) per test case, where m ≤ 10^6 and k ≤ 20 (practically ≤ 2·10^7 elementary operations).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(m + k^2) – an array of size m for cycle detection and a (k+1)×(k+1) matrix.
